what_it_does: This file is an AGENTS ledger for the top-level full-screen app states under src/screens, describing each screen's role, how the wizard hosts the unified StreamRunner, the intake feedback layer, supporting plain-JS logic files, and local conventions.
observations: welcome.jsx is the front door with a Vi greeting, open prompt, and recent projects
projects.jsx is a project picker grid
templates.jsx is a template gallery plus detail view
wizard.jsx is a thin host around the unified StreamRunner running the init stream, exporting StepDirection/Renderer/Sequence/Ready, Deductions, and MoodTile to window for the runner
intake.jsx is a live conversational interview that mounts the feedback layer
intake-engine.js, intake-ai.js, and synth-ai.js hold plain-JS logic and mock data behind intake/wizard
palette DATA in those JS files may contain literal hex because it feeds colour math, while rendered output still uses tokens
screens are window.XScreen-style components, fixed-position full-bleed, reflowing via styles/responsive.css
entrance choreography is centralized in styles/choreo.css
status uses Chip/MS_STATUS, sizing uses tokens, wordmark uses ViMark
standouts: wizard.jsx is intentionally a thin host around the unified StreamRunner for the init stream, exporting renderer components to window
intake.jsx mounts a feedback layer for live conversational interviewing
literal hex in palette data files is explicitly allowed as a definition that feeds colour math, not a duplicate to eliminate
screens are full-screen routed states distinct from studio-shell workspaces
conventions: Screens are fixed-position full-bleed and rely on styles/responsive.css for reflow
Entrance choreography is not hand-rolled per screen; it lives in styles/choreo.css
Rendered UI uses tokens; raw palette data in plain-JS logic files may use literal hex
Status is communicated via Chip/MS_STATUS, sizing via tokens, wordmark via ViMark
Plain-JS logic and mock data are separated into intake-engine.js, intake-ai.js, and synth-ai.js
concerns: The wizard host/runner contract is described only at a high level, so the exact integration surface is not visible here
The literal-hex exception for palette data could be mistaken for a token violation if the boundary is not clearly enforced
No explicit routing table or app.jsx mapping is shown in this file
notes: A screen-level orientation doc that frames wizard as a StreamRunner host and intake as a feedback-layer interview, with strict token/rendering conventions.
questions: What is the exact contract between wizard.jsx and the unified StreamRunner in src/workspaces/streams.jsx?
How does app.jsx route these window.XScreen components?
What is the feedback layer that intake.jsx mounts?
What are MS_STATUS and the token naming conventions?
How does the init stream map to the wizard's StepDirection/Renderer/Sequence/Ready components?
What is ViMark and where is it defined?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to document top-level app states and conventions, which matches what the file does.
apparent_themes: Separation of full-screen app states from in-shell workspaces
Thin-host architecture delegating flow to a shared runner
Centralized styling and choreography over per-screen custom transforms
Data definitions may differ from rendered tokens when they feed computation
intention_signals: It aims to give maintainers a single orientation point for screen-level responsibilities, emphasizing that the wizard is not a standalone flow but a host for a reusable init stream, and that intake is a live interview surface.
novelty: The wizard-as-thin-host around a unified StreamRunner, and the explicit carve-out that literal hex in palette data is a definition rather than a duplicate.
contribution: It documents the screen-level topology, the wizard-as-host abstraction, the intake/wizard logic split, and the convention that raw palette data may contain literal hex while rendered output uses tokens.
summary_for_embedding: AGENTS ledger for src/screens documenting full-screen app states, the wizard as a StreamRunner host, intake feedback layer, supporting plain-JS logic files, and styling/routing conventions.
intention_for_embedding: This file was written to orient maintainers on the responsibilities and integration boundaries of the top-level screens in the Counterpart app.
